one_line_takeaway: ICON detects indirect prompt injection via an attention-entropy 'over-focusing' signature (Focus Intensity Score) and neutralizes it with inference-time attention steering rather than refusal, matching a fine-tuned guardrail's ~0.2-0.4% ASR while recovering far more task utility (e.g., 53.8% vs <49% UA on Qwen-3-8B) and training in under 2 minutes on 255 samples.
key_finding: On the three text backbones (Table 2), averaged across attack types, ICON achieves 0.4% average ASR versus 32.7% (No Defense), 16.8% (Repeat Prompt), 21.8% (Delimiting), 3.2% (MELON), and 0.2% (LLM-Detector/Qwen3Guard) -- essentially matching the strongest fine-tuned guardrail baseline on security. On utility, ICON achieves 53.8% average UA on Qwen-3-8B versus the next-best defense below 49%, and the paper reports this as roughly a 10% average UA improvement over other defenses and a >50% utility gain relative to refusal-based approaches cited in the abstract/conclusion. Against MELON specifically, ICON reduces ASR by an additional ~3 percentage points while providing a ~10-point UA boost. In the OOD generalization experiment (Table 3, prober trained only on TrojanTools), ICON reaches Attack Detection Rate of 98.0% on AgentDojo and 80.1% on InjectAgent (Qwen-3-8B), with Utility Recovery Rate of 69.6% (AgentDojo) and 62.3% (InjectAgent); Llama-3.1-8B shows ADR of 97.3% (AgentDojo) / 85.4% (InjectAgent) with URR of 55.9% / 47.6%. Efficiency (Table 4): ICON trains in under 2 minutes on 255 samples with ~31,553 parameters, versus Qwen3Guard's >10 hours of fine-tuning on 1.19M samples with 8B parameters; MELON and Repeat Prompt have zero training cost but are static/heuristic. On multimodal agents (Table 5), ICON achieves 2.9% average ASR (vs. 69.0% no-defense, 28.1% Repeat Prompt) while maintaining 47.2% average UA, compared to the Gemini-based LLM-Detector's 0.0% ASR but only 28.8% UA -- the paper frames this ~20-point UA gap as evidence the commercial detector over-refuses.
core_contribution: ICON is an inference-time, training-free-at-deployment defense against indirect prompt injection (IPI) attacks on LLM agents. It combines three components introduced by the authors: (1) the Focus Intensity Score (FIS), a per-head/per-layer metric derived from generation-normalized attention entropy that identifies which attention heads exhibit abnormal 'over-focusing' on a small subset of context tokens; (2) the Latent Space Trace Prober (LSTP), a lightweight CNN+MLP classifier trained on FIS-derived features (min/mean/std of entropy per head, concatenated across a subset of attack-sensitive layers, then adaptive max-pooled) that detects IPI attacks from attention-weight signatures rather than surface text; and (3) the Mitigating Rectifier (MR), which, upon detection, performs 'surgical' attention steering on the flagged heads before the softmax -- suppressing attention above a percentile threshold (steering scope tau) by a contrastive multiplicative factor (steering intensity gamma) -- to redistribute attention away from the injected payload and back toward benign task context, allowing the agent to continue its trajectory instead of refusing. The paper also introduces an offline adaptive-attack data synthesis module (an LLM-as-Optimizer loop against a formalized attacker objective) used only to generate training data for the prober/rectifier, not as an attack contribution in itself.
threat_model: Grey-box adversary with no access to the target LLM's weights or architecture but able to infer agent context via intermediate servers. Attacker constraints as defined in the paper: (1) external injection -- payload delta can only be inserted into the observation stream O_t via external sources such as compromised MCP servers or retrieval corpora, not by directly controlling the user instruction; (2) context awareness -- attacker has partial task knowledge (e.g., shopping, payment) to make delta semantically consistent with benign content and evade surface-level filters; (3) malicious redirection -- attacker's goal is to steer the agent toward an unauthorized tool call f_adv while keeping the injected trace stealthy in latent space. The defender is the white-box system operator with access to the local LLM's internals (hidden states, attention weights) at inference time; the defender's goal is formalized as a dual objective of minimizing detection error (prober loss) while maximizing task-completion log-probability under the rectification operator, without updating model weights.
limitations: The paper's own text surfaces several caveats rather than presenting these as external criticism: (1) The defense requires white-box access to model internals (hidden states, attention weights) at inference time, explicitly stated as part of the defender's capability assumption -- it is not applicable to closed/API-only models. (2) The offline data-synthesis and prober-training pipeline still depends on generating a representative set of adaptive attacks (TrojanTools-style, LLM-as-Optimizer loop) to calibrate FIS-sensitive layers/heads and rectifier hyperparameters (tau, gamma); the paper does not report sensitivity of results to poor coverage in this synthesis step, nor an ablation isolating the LSTP detector's standalone accuracy from the combined pipeline's ASR/UA outcome. (3) Baseline comparisons for the fine-tuning-based upper bound use Qwen3Guard (text) and Gemini (multimodal) as "the most advanced (M)LLM currently available" -- the paper does not test against more recent or diverse guardrail models beyond these two. (4) The visualization in Section 5.4 is illustrative (single head/layer range, Layer 22-25 Head 21 on Qwen-3-8B) rather than a systematic study of how consistently the identified over-focusing signature holds across models/layers. (5) No adaptive-attack evaluation against ICON itself is reported (i.e., an attacker aware of FIS-based detection and explicitly optimizing to evade it) -- the TrojanTools attacks used for training/eval were not observed to be constructed with knowledge of ICON's own detection mechanism.

datasets_used: TrojanTools (Anonymous, 2025) - adaptive IPI benchmark via malicious tool-calling, used for training
InjecAgent (Zhan et al., 2024) - used for OOD evaluation
AgentDojo (Debenedetti et al., 2024) - used for OOD evaluation
Visual Prompt Injection Benchmarks (Wan et al., 2024, CyberSecEval 3, provided by Meta) - used for multimodal evaluation
quantitative_results: Average ASR across 3 text backbones: ICON 0.4% vs No Defense 32.7%, Repeat Prompt 16.8%, Delimiting 21.8%, MELON 3.2%, LLM-Detector(Qwen3Guard) 0.2% (Table 2)
Qwen-3-8B: ICON UA 53.8% vs next-best defense <49% UA, with ASR 0.4%
vs MELON: ICON reduces ASR by an additional ~3 points and boosts UA by ~10 points
OOD (Qwen-3-8B, trained only on TrojanTools): ADR 98.0%±1.2 on AgentDojo, 80.1%±1.9 on InjectAgent; URR 69.6%±2.7 on AgentDojo, 62.3%±2.3 on InjectAgent (Table 3)
OOD (Llama-3.1-8B): ADR 97.3%±1.8 on AgentDojo, 85.4%±1.3 on InjectAgent; URR 55.9%±3.3 on AgentDojo, 47.6%±3.1 on InjectAgent (Table 3)
Efficiency: ICON trains in <2 minutes on 255 samples with ~31,553 parameters vs Qwen3Guard >10 hours on 1.19M samples, 8B parameters (Table 4)
Multimodal (avg across Qwen3-VL, InternVL, MiniCPM): ICON ASR 2.9%, UA 47.2%, vs No Defense ASR 69.0%/UA 59.0%, Repeat Prompt ASR 28.1%/UA 49.7%, LLM-Detector(Gemini) ASR 0.0%/UA 28.8% (Table 5)
baselines_compared: LLM-Detector: Qwen3Guard (text agents) / Gemini (multimodal agents) - fine-tuning-based defense
